title: Picea bracteata
taxonomy: Picea bracteata is classified under the kingdom Plantae, with further classification as follows: it belongs to the division Pinophyta, the class Pinopsida, the order Pinales, and the family Pinaceae. Within the family Pinaceae, Picea bracteata is part of the genus Picea. This classification indicates that Picea bracteata is a type of evergreen conifer tree. The classification is based on various characteristics, including its needle-like leaves and conical shape. The classification helps to identify and distinguish Picea bracteata from other plant species.
uses: Picea bracteata has been traditionally used by the indigenous peoples of Mexico for various medicinal purposes. The resin of the plant has been reported to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. It has also been used to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and coughs. Additionally, the plant has been used to treat skin conditions, such as eczema and acne. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of these uses.

intro: Picea bracteata, also known as Apache pine, is a coniferous tree native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The resin and oil extracted from the tree's bark and needles are valued for their potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties, making it useful in treating various skin conditions and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Picea bracteata include terpenes, such as limonene and pinene, as well as phenolic compounds, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the resin is often used to make salves, ointments, and inhalants, which are used to treat conditions such as eczema, wounds, and bronchitis.
